content:
type: REVISE
my_files: outlines/prompts.py
your_files: outlines/prompts.py
note: We both need this one file - let's split by function/region instead of by file. You own: adding a 'filters' param to prompt()/from_str()/from_file()/Prompt (signature changes, plumbing local filters into the Jinja Environment). I own: new standalone register_filter()/unregister_filter() functions plus a module-level _FILTER_REGISTRY dict, and I'll add ONE line in each env-building spot (_template_from_str/_template_from_file) that does env.filters.update(_FILTER_REGISTRY) BEFORE any local filters are applied, so local filters (yours) naturally take precedence since they'll be applied after. Please apply your local 'filters' param update to env.filters AFTER the built-in filter assignments so precedence holds. Do you want to just take the whole file yourself instead since edits are so intertwined, or is this split workable for you?

content:
type: ACCEPT
my_files: outlines/prompts.py
your_files: outlines/prompts.py
note: We're in separate sandboxes each producing our own patch.txt, so there's no literal shared-file clobbering risk - let's each implement independently but agree on a compatible convention so the two features compose cleanly if ever merged. Spec for my part: module-level dict _FILTER_REGISTRY: Dict[str, Callable] = {}; register_filter(name: str, filter_func: Callable) -> None sets _FILTER_REGISTRY[name] = filter_func and refreshes env filters; unregister_filter(name: str) -> None pops it. Hook point: in _template_from_str and _template_from_file, right after the existing built-in env.filters[...] assignments, I add . Convention for your part: apply your local  dict via  AFTER that line, so local overrides global/registry as required. Proceeding to implement now on my end.

content:
type: ACCEPT
my_files: outlines/prompts.py
note: Exact spec: register_filter(name: str, filter_func: Callable) -> None - stores _FILTER_REGISTRY[name] = filter_func, overwriting silently if name already registered (no exception), returns None. unregister_filter(name: str) -> None - removes name from _FILTER_REGISTRY via dict.pop(name, None), silent no-op if not present, returns None. Both are module-level functions in outlines/prompts.py alongside a module-level _FILTER_REGISTRY: Dict[str, Callable] = {}. One clarification: we are each working in our own isolated repo/sandbox - I do not have filesystem access to your copy and you do not have access to mine, so there is no actual mechanism for either of us to send/apply a real diff to the other's checkout, or to truly 'own' a shared file. Whatever each of us implements only affects our own patch.txt. So please go ahead and implement your filters-param feature however makes sense in your own copy, and I will implement my registry feature (per the spec above) in mine, using the precedence convention (registry applied first, local filters applied after/last) so the designs are conceptually compatible if a human later merges both PRs. No further diff exchange needed. Marking this DONE on my end shortly.

content:
type: DONE
my_files: outlines/prompts.py
note: Implemented and tested: module-level _FILTER_REGISTRY dict plus register_filter(name, filter_func) and unregister_filter(name) functions. Hooked env.filters.update(_FILTER_REGISTRY) into both _template_from_str and _template_from_file, right before the built-in filter assignments, so registry filters are available to all new prompt templates but do not clobber built-ins, and can still be overridden by any locally-provided filters applied afterward (your part). Verified with pytest tests/test_prompts.py (13 passed) plus manual checks that register_filter makes a filter usable in both @prompt-decorated functions and Prompt.from_str, and that unregister_filter removes it for newly-created templates. Diff written to my patch.txt. Good luck with your part!
